q: What is a feature in machine learning?
a: A feature is one piece of input information the model uses to make a prediction. For predicting house price, features might be size, number of rooms, and location.
fs: A feature is an input signal the model learns from; good features carry the patterns the model needs, and feature quality usually matters more than the algorithm.
fj: Confusing a feature (an input) with the label (the answer you want to predict); the model learns to map features to the label.

q: What is a model in machine learning?
a: A model is the thing that learns patterns from data and then makes predictions on new data. You feed it examples, it adjusts itself, and afterward it can guess answers it has not seen.
fs: A model is a function with learned parameters that maps inputs to outputs; training fits those parameters to data so the model generalizes to new inputs.
fj: Thinking the model 'memorizes' the training data; the goal is to learn general patterns that work on new, unseen examples.

a: Training is the process of showing the model many labeled examples so it can learn the patterns between inputs and the correct answers. Over many passes, it tweaks itself to make fewer mistakes.
fs: Training adjusts model parameters to minimize a loss on the training data, gradually reducing prediction error step by step.
fj: Assuming training happens once and is instant; it is usually many repeated passes, and more data or steps does not always mean better results.

code.label: the training loop idea
lines: 3. nudge the model to do better

a: Inference is when you use an already-trained model to get an answer for a brand-new input. Training is the learning phase; inference is the using phase.
fs: Inference runs the trained model forward on fresh inputs to produce predictions; in production, inference speed, cost, and reliability are the main concerns.
fj: Mixing up training and inference; during inference the model is not learning, it just applies what it already learned.

a: Overfitting is when a model learns the training examples too closely, including their noise, so it does great on training data but poorly on new data. It memorized instead of learning the real pattern.
fs: Overfitting is low training error but high error on unseen data; you catch it by checking a separate validation/test set, not the training set.
fj: Trusting high training accuracy as proof the model is good; only performance on data it has never seen tells you the truth.
